quantum-b01.text: What did NIST's multi-year post-quantum competition produce?
options: Standardized algorithms (FIPS 203/204/205) to replace quantum-vulnerable crypto
quantum-b01.explanation: The ~8-year process selected and standardized the PQC algorithms securing the next era.

quantum-b01.text: Why run an open, multi-year competition for PQC?
options: Public cryptanalysis builds confidence the algorithms resist attack
quantum-b01.explanation: Open scrutiny is how cryptographic trust is earned — several candidates were broken during it.

quantum-b01.text: The standardized PQC algorithms are based mainly on…
options: Lattice problems (and hash-based signatures) Shor's can't break
quantum-b01.explanation: PQC rests on lattice and hash problems, not the factoring/discrete-log Shor's defeats.

quantum-b01.text: Which trio did the competition standardize?
quantum-b01.explanation: ML-KEM (key exchange) plus ML-DSA and SLH-DSA (signatures) form the core suite.

quantum-b01.text: Why standardize multiple signature algorithms (ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, FALCON)?
options: Algorithm diversity hedges against a future break in any one family
quantum-b01.explanation: Different mathematical bases provide a fallback if one is broken.

options: They're the vetted replacements that will secure the internet post-quantum
quantum-b01.explanation: They are the foundation of post-quantum security going forward.

options: Begin adopting them, starting with key exchange
quantum-b01.explanation: Finalized standards mean migration can and should start.

quantum-b01.text: Some competition candidates (like SIKE) were broken during the process. The lesson is…
options: Real-world cryptanalysis is essential before trusting an algorithm
quantum-b01.explanation: Public attack attempts weeded out weak candidates before standardization.

quantum-b02.text: What is the Learning With Errors (LWE) problem?
options: A lattice problem that's hard for both classical and quantum computers
quantum-b02.explanation: LWE (and its variants) is the bedrock hardness assumption for lattice PQC.

quantum-b02.text: Why is LWE believed to resist quantum attack?
options: No efficient quantum algorithm is known for lattice problems like LWE
quantum-b02.explanation: Unlike factoring/discrete-log, lattice problems have no known quantum break.

quantum-b02.text: What role does the added 'error' (noise) play in LWE?
options: It makes recovering the secret computationally hard
quantum-b02.explanation: Small random errors turn easy linear algebra into a hard problem.

quantum-b02.text: Which standardized algorithms are built on lattice/LWE-style problems?
Lattice cryptography's hardness comes from problems like…
options: Finding the shortest/closest vector in a high-dimensional lattice

quantum-b02.text: LWE is attractive for PQC because it's…
options: Hard for classical and quantum computers, yet efficient to implement
quantum-b02.explanation: It combines strong security with practical performance.

quantum-b02.text: Why is LWE trusted as a foundation?
options: It has withstood decades of cryptanalysis with no efficient attack found
quantum-b02.explanation: Long, intense study without a break builds confidence.

quantum-b02.text: Moving to lattice-based crypto changes the security assumption from…
quantum-b02.explanation: PQC swaps the quantum-vulnerable assumption for a quantum-resistant one.

options: Post-quantum key encapsulation — the drop-in replacement for RSA/ECDH key exchange
quantum-b03.explanation: ML-KEM establishes shared keys, replacing quantum-vulnerable key exchange.

quantum-b03.text: A Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM) is used to…
options: Securely establish a shared symmetric key between two parties
quantum-b03.explanation: A KEM produces a shared secret; ML-KEM is the PQC standard for it.

quantum-b03.text: Why is migrating key exchange to ML-KEM the most urgent PQC step?
options: It immediately stops Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later capture of new traffic
quantum-b03.explanation: PQC key exchange protects today's traffic from future decryption.
Kyber/ML-KEM is a lattice-based scheme, safe from Shor's.

quantum-b03.text: How is ML-KEM typically deployed in TLS during migration?
options: As a hybrid with a classical algorithm (e.g. X25519 + ML-KEM)
quantum-b03.explanation: Hybrid key exchange hedges against flaws in either component during transition.

options: Fills the same role (key establishment) without the quantum vulnerability
quantum-b03.explanation: It plays RSA/ECDH's role with quantum-resistant math.

quantum-b03.text: Why is ML-KEM called the most important PQC algorithm?
options: Key exchange secures nearly every encrypted connection on the internet
It's the only one that exists
quantum-b03.explanation: Securing key exchange protects the bulk of internet traffic.
FIPS 203 is ML-KEM; 204 is ML-DSA; 205 is SLH-DSA.

quantum-b04.explanation: ML-DSA is the primary post-quantum signature standard.

quantum-b04.text: Where are post-quantum signatures like ML-DSA needed?
options: TLS certificates, code signing, and PKI
quantum-b04.explanation: Signatures authenticate certs, software, and identities — all need PQC.
Like ML-KEM, ML-DSA is lattice-based and quantum-resistant.

quantum-b04.text: Why are signatures generally lower HNDL urgency than key exchange?
options: A forged signature requires a quantum computer now; harvested key exchange can be broken later
quantum-b04.explanation: HNDL targets confidentiality (key exchange); signature forgery needs a CRQC at attack time.

quantum-b04.text: A practical migration challenge with PQC signatures is…
options: Larger key/signature sizes that stress certificates and protocols
quantum-b04.explanation: PQC keys/signatures are bigger, affecting cert chains and packet sizes.

quantum-b04.text: Why migrate signatures to PQC at all if HNDL is the bigger near-term risk?
options: After Q-Day, a CRQC could forge any RSA/ECDSA signature, breaking trust
quantum-b04.explanation: Eventually all classical signatures become forgeable; PKI must migrate too.

options: The general-purpose default PQC signature for most applications
A backup never to be used
quantum-b04.explanation: ML-DSA is the recommended default signature algorithm.

quantum-b05.text: What is SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+, FIPS 205) based on?
options: The security of hash functions alone
quantum-b05.explanation: SLH-DSA relies only on hash security — the simplest assumption.

quantum-b05.text: Why is SLH-DSA called the most conservative PQC signature?
options: Hash-function security is extremely well-understood and minimal-assumption
quantum-b05.explanation: Depending only on hashes means fewer assumptions that could fail.

quantum-b05.text: What's the main downside of SLH-DSA?
options: Large signature sizes and slower signing than lattice schemes
quantum-b05.explanation: Hash-based signatures are big and slower — the price of conservatism.

quantum-b05.text: SLH-DSA is a good choice when…
options: You want maximum long-term assurance and can tolerate larger signatures (e.g. firmware/root signing)
You need the smallest possible signature
quantum-b05.explanation: Its conservative security suits high-assurance, infrequent signing like roots/firmware.

quantum-b05.text: Why standardize SLH-DSA alongside lattice signatures?
options: If a lattice break is found, a hash-based fallback remains
quantum-b05.explanation: A different mathematical basis is a hedge against a lattice break.

quantum-b05.text: The appeal of hash-based signatures is…
options: You only have to trust your hash function, which is heavily studied

quantum-b05.text: Having both lattice and hash-based signatures gives the ecosystem…
options: Resilience — a fallback if one family is ever broken
quantum-b05.explanation: Algorithm diversity is defense in depth for cryptography.

quantum-b06.text: What's FALCON's standout property among NIST PQC signatures?
options: The smallest signature sizes of the standardized signatures

quantum-b06.text: What's the cost of FALCON's small signatures?
options: Complex, subtle implementation (floating-point/Gaussian sampling) that's easy to get wrong
quantum-b06.explanation: FALCON's math is delicate to implement correctly and constant-time.

quantum-b06.text: Why is FALCON's implementation difficulty a security concern?
options: Subtle errors (e.g. non-constant-time floating point) can leak the private key
quantum-b06.explanation: Implementation flaws in its sampling can leak secrets via side channels.

options: Bandwidth/size is tightly constrained and you can implement it carefully
quantum-b06.explanation: Its small signatures suit size-constrained protocols — if implemented well.
FALCON is a lattice-based signature over NTRU lattices.

quantum-b06.text: Compared to ML-DSA, FALCON is best seen as…
options: A specialist choice for small signatures, vs ML-DSA the general default
quantum-b06.explanation: ML-DSA is the easy default; FALCON is for size-critical cases.

options: An algorithm is only as secure as its implementation
quantum-b06.explanation: Correct, constant-time implementation is essential — especially for FALCON.
You gain small signatures but take on implementation complexity.

quantum-b07.text: What is a hybrid post-quantum scheme?
options: Combining a classical algorithm with a PQC one so it's secure if either holds
quantum-b07.explanation: Hybrid combines e.g. X25519 + ML-KEM for defense in depth.

options: It stays secure even if the new PQC algorithm has an undiscovered flaw
quantum-b07.explanation: Hybrid protects against both quantum attacks and immature-PQC bugs.

quantum-b07.text: Which is a real hybrid key exchange?
quantum-b07.explanation: Combining a classical ECDH (X25519) with ML-KEM is the common hybrid.

quantum-b07.text: A hybrid scheme is secure as long as…
options: At least one of the two algorithms remains unbroken
quantum-b07.explanation: An attacker must break both components to win.

options: Larger handshakes and a bit more compute from running two algorithms
quantum-b07.explanation: You pay some size/compute overhead for the extra assurance.

quantum-b07.text: Hybrid is recommended for the transition period because…
options: PQC is new; hybrid avoids putting all trust in an unproven algorithm
quantum-b07.explanation: It's a prudent bridge while PQC matures in the field.

options: Defense in depth — don't rely on a single mechanism
quantum-b07.explanation: Layering two independent algorithms is defense in depth for crypto.

options: Already deployed in major browsers/servers for TLS
quantum-b07.explanation: Hybrid (e.g. X25519+ML-KEM) is live in real TLS today.

quantum-b08.text: Why is migrating TLS to post-quantum significant?
options: TLS is the most deployed security protocol on earth — it touches every layer
quantum-b08.explanation: TLS secures most internet traffic, so its migration is foundational and far-reaching.

quantum-b08.text: What's the first part of TLS to make post-quantum?
options: The key exchange (via hybrid ML-KEM) to stop HNDL
quantum-b08.explanation: PQC key exchange protects confidentiality against harvesting immediately.

quantum-b08.text: Why is migrating TLS certificates/signatures harder than key exchange?
options: It requires updating the whole PKI/CA chain and handling larger signatures
quantum-b08.explanation: The PKI ecosystem and larger PQC signatures make cert migration a bigger lift.

options: Larger PQC keys/signatures can exceed packet sizes and slow handshakes
quantum-b08.explanation: PQC artifacts are bigger, stressing handshake performance and packet limits.

quantum-b08.text: The recommended TLS migration approach is…
options: Hybrid key exchange first, then PQC certificates as PKI catches up
quantum-b08.explanation: Phase it: protect confidentiality first, migrate authentication as the ecosystem matures.

quantum-b08.text: 'Changes at every layer' means TLS PQC migration touches…
options: Clients, servers, libraries, CAs, and middleboxes
quantum-b08.explanation: The whole stack must support the new algorithms and sizes.

quantum-b08.text: Why can network middleboxes complicate PQC TLS?
options: They may reject larger or unfamiliar handshakes (protocol ossification)
quantum-b08.explanation: Ossified middleboxes can break larger/novel handshakes — a real deployment hurdle.

quantum-b08.text: TLS PQC migration is a priority because…
options: Securing the most-used protocol protects the bulk of internet traffic from HNDL
quantum-b08.explanation: TLS's ubiquity makes its migration the highest-leverage move.

quantum-b09.text: Why is cryptographic discovery the most important PQC migration step?
options: You can't migrate crypto you haven't found
quantum-b09.explanation: A complete inventory is the prerequisite for any migration plan.

options: Apps, libraries, firmware, protocols, certificates, and hardcoded keys
quantum-b09.explanation: RSA/ECC is embedded throughout the stack — discovery is genuinely hard.

quantum-b09.text: What does a Cryptographic Bill of Materials (CBOM) capture?
options: Where each cryptographic algorithm/key is used across the org
quantum-b09.explanation: A CBOM is the crypto inventory that drives prioritized migration.

quantum-b09.text: Why is crypto discovery so often underestimated?
options: Crypto is pervasive and frequently buried in dependencies and legacy systems
quantum-b09.explanation: Hidden, embedded crypto makes discovery a major, ongoing effort.

options: It reveals which assets hold long-lived secrets most exposed to HNDL
quantum-b09.explanation: Knowing where crypto lives lets you migrate the highest-risk assets first.

options: Mapping crypto so algorithms can be swapped quickly when needed
quantum-b09.explanation: An inventory is the foundation of being able to change algorithms fast.

options: Code/binary scanning, network analysis, and certificate/dependency inventory
quantum-b09.explanation: Multiple techniques are needed to find crypto across the environment.

quantum-b09.text: Before any PQC migration plan, you must…
options: Know what crypto you have and where (the inventory)
quantum-b09.explanation: Inventory is step zero of every credible migration roadmap.

quantum-b10.text: Why isn't choosing a standardized PQC algorithm enough?
options: A PQC algorithm is only as secure as its implementation
quantum-b10.explanation: Validate, test, and audit the implementation — not just the algorithm choice.

quantum-b10.text: What implementation flaw can leak a PQC private key?
options: Non-constant-time operations that leak via timing/power side channels
quantum-b10.explanation: Side channels (e.g. timing) can leak secrets even from a sound algorithm.

quantum-b10.text: How should PQC implementations be validated?
options: Use vetted libraries, run test vectors, and pursue FIPS validation
Write your own from scratch and trust it
quantum-b10.explanation: Known-answer tests and FIPS validation catch implementation errors.

quantum-b10.text: For PQC, the 'don't roll your own crypto' rule means…
options: Prefer well-reviewed, maintained libraries over custom implementations
quantum-b10.explanation: Subtle PQC details make hand-rolled implementations risky.

quantum-b10.text: Which standardized signature is notoriously hard to implement safely?
quantum-b10.explanation: FALCON's sampling is easy to get wrong, risking key leakage.

quantum-b10.text: Known-answer test vectors are used to…
options: Confirm an implementation matches the standard's expected outputs
quantum-b10.explanation: Test vectors verify the implementation is correct against the spec.

quantum-b10.text: Where should PQC implementations come from?
quantum-b10.explanation: Supply-chain trust matters as much for PQC as for any crypto.

options: Validate, test, and audit before trusting any PQC code in production
quantum-b10.explanation: Sound deployment requires verifying the implementation, not just the algorithm.
